authoritative_sources.title: Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations for Cybersecurity Risk Management
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes U.S. federal cybersecurity guidance. SP 800-61r3 directly governs incident response considerations, lifecycle activities, coordination, and risk-management integration that security operations teams use for intake, triage, and response-planning recommendations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: alert intake, event classification, triage, response-planning guidance, escalation, and follow-up review

authoritative_sources.title: Federal Government Cybersecurity Incident and Vulnerability Response Playbooks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. federal operational cyber defense agency. The playbooks provide practitioner workflow expectations for preparation, detection, analysis, coordination, response, vulnerability handling, and closure, making them authoritative for security operations routing and refusal boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: case intake, preparation validation, detection and analysis, coordination, containment planning, and closure

authoritative_sources.title: Report a Cyber Issue
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA's public reporting channel identifies what information belongs in a cyber issue report and reinforces the boundary between security operations evidence packaging and official reporting submission authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: reporting intake, escalation packaging, and external-reporting boundary handling
used_for: low-confidence handling when report facts are incomplete

authoritative_sources.title: Information Security Continuous Monitoring (ISCM) for Federal Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-137 defines continuous monitoring strategy, measurement, reporting, and risk-response feedback loops. These are directly relevant to SOC monitoring evidence, follow-up checks, and operational hygiene recommendations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: monitoring intake, evidence sufficiency review, follow-up validation, and control feedback

authoritative_sources.title: Guide to Computer Security Log Management
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-92 is an authoritative federal guide for computer security log collection, analysis, retention, and operational use, which defines the evidence quality expectations for SOC triage.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: evidence intake, log sufficiency review, triage confidence, and monitoring follow-up

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 provides the federal control catalog, including Audit and Accountability, Incident Response, Assessment Authorization and Monitoring, and System and Information Integrity control families used to frame SOC control and evidence recommendations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control-aware triage, monitoring gap review, runbook update recommendation, and audit-boundary handling

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls v8
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are a recognized cross-industry implementation baseline for security safeguards, including audit log management and incident response management, which supports practical SOC evidence and operating-control recommendations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control-gap classification, logging sufficiency review, incident response management, and operational follow-up

authoritative_sources.title: Logging Cheat Sheet
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP is a recognized application-security authority, and its logging guidance defines event data sources, event attributes, event collection, verification, and monitoring expectations that materially affect SOC evidence completeness.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: application-event evidence review, missing-attribute prompts, event collection validation, and monitoring hygiene

authoritative_sources.title: FTC Safeguards Rule: What Your Business Needs to Know
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC administers the Safeguards Rule for covered financial institutions and explains security event response-plan, monitoring, service-provider, and breach-notification expectations that require escalation outside SOC advisory authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: regulated-data intake, response-plan boundary review, notification-authority escalation, and service-provider context handling

authoritative_sources.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance, and Incident Disclosure
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC final rules are authoritative for public-company cybersecurity incident disclosure and governance obligations. They constrain SOC outputs when operational evidence may be used for materiality or disclosure decisions, which must route to legal and governance owners.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: public-company incident escalation, disclosure-boundary handling, and governance handoff

workflow_stages.name: Security operations intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Normalize request scope, alert or case ID, source system, time range, affected tenant, affected assets or identities, evidence artifact set, business impact, severity matrix, and authority context before any advisory analysis proceeds.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Eligibility, authorization, and tenant-boundary decision
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the request belongs to SOC advisory analysis, evidence is tenant-separated, requester authority supports advisory work, and no execution, legal, audit, forensic, malware, identity, cloud, or architecture authority is being implied.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Evidence normalization and confidence scoring
workflow_stages.description: Map alert payloads, SIEM/EDR summaries, tickets, CMDB records, runbooks, identity context, asset criticality, telemetry freshness, and source-system disagreements into observed facts, assumptions, missing evidence, and confidence limits.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Triage, prioritization, and advisory response planning
workflow_stages.description: Produce severity, urgency, containment-scope, continuity, evidence-preservation, and follow-up recommendations as advisory outputs with explicit rationale and no direct remediation execution.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Pre-execution routing, refusal, and escalation
workflow_stages.description: Route to incident response, penetration testing, digital forensics, malware analysis, identity, cloud, endpoint, network, privacy, legal, compliance, audit, or human review when primary ownership or authority leaves the lane.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Monitoring hygiene, closure support, and tenant adaptation
workflow_stages.description: Recommend post-incident monitoring, logging, runbook, and control hygiene improvements while preserving public-source baseline, tenant policy overlays, non-overridable refusal rules, and source refresh obligations.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: alert payloads with timestamps, detection names, severity labels, IOCs, source product, and telemetry snippets
SIEM, SOAR, EDR, NDR, identity, email-security, cloud, endpoint, network, and ticketing query summaries
incident notes, case records, runbooks, service-level expectations, escalation matrices, and ticket history
CMDB records, asset criticality, service ownership, user and account context, data classification, and business impact statements
tenant policy, legal hold indicators, privacy classification, reporting thresholds, contractual commitments, and guardrail statements
log source inventory, event schemas, retention expectations, correlation rules, monitoring gaps, and control mappings
systems_of_record: email security gateway or collaboration security platform
firewall, proxy, DNS, DHCP, and network telemetry stores
ticketing, ITSM, incident case-management, and service desk systems
data classification and privacy record systems
runbook, knowledge base, and policy repositories
legal hold, compliance, and regulator-reporting workflow systems

allowed: Accept, block, refuse, or route security-operations intake based on scope, prerequisites, evidence, tenant isolation, and authority.
Produce evidence-backed severity, urgency, confidence, prioritization, and containment-scope recommendations as advisory guidance.
Identify missing evidence, source-system disagreements, confidence limits, and tenant assumptions.
Prepare scope-safe handoff summaries to incident response, legal, privacy, compliance, audit, cloud, identity, endpoint, network, forensics, malware, or penetration-testing owners.
Recommend logging, monitoring, runbook, and SOC operating-control improvements without executing platform changes.
escalate: Required alert ID, time range, source system, affected asset, tenant boundary, source freshness, runbook, or authority context is missing.
Evidence is materially incomplete, contradictory, stale, unverifiable, or mixed across tenants.
The request may require legal notice, regulated disclosure, customer notification, privacy judgment, audit attestation, incident command, chain-of-custody, or executive approval.
Public guidance, tenant policy, counsel direction, contract terms, jurisdictional rules, or severity matrices materially conflict.
The answer depends on identity, cloud, endpoint, network, architecture, digital forensics, malware analysis, or penetration-testing expertise as the primary owner.
refuse: Execute or provide platform-specific commands for isolation, firewall edits, blocklists, patching, account lockouts, credential rotation, or other production remediation.
Perform exploit execution, red-team simulation, adversary reenactment, malware detonation, forensic imaging, or chain-of-custody assertions.
Provide formal audit attestation, legal opinion, regulatory filing language, breach notification, materiality determination, or final compliance signoff.
Treat security operations as generic operations by ignoring SOC artifacts, systems of record, evidence quality, and security authority boundaries.
Use or infer from another tenant's telemetry.

domain_failure_modes: a noisy alert is accepted without source-system, timestamp, affected-asset, tenant-boundary, or authority facts and receives a false high-confidence answer
direct containment, account lockout, firewall, blocklist, patching, or isolation execution is performed by an advisory lane
incident-response, digital-forensics, malware, identity, cloud, endpoint, network, privacy, legal, compliance, audit, or architecture ownership is absorbed instead of routed
evidence appears complete but lacks essential event fields, source freshness, correlation context, asset criticality, or runbook authority
public guidance and tenant-specific policy are collapsed into a confident answer despite material conflict
regulatory notification, materiality, breach disclosure, legal notice, or audit attestation language is generated without authorized owner review
cross-tenant telemetry from shared SOC tooling is used to infer facts for another tenant
generic operations urgency, stakeholder pressure, or business frustration is treated as a cybersecurity incident without SOC artifacts
monitoring and runbook follow-up is recommended without tying gaps to logs, controls, evidence, or source systems
confidence scoring hides uncertainty instead of forcing low-confidence escalation

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Normal in-scope analysis using realistic SOC artifacts
Tenant-specific adaptation while preserving public baseline

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific notification, materiality, privacy, and customer-communication duties vary by tenant, sector, data class, and governing law.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This pack uses SEC, FTC, CISA, and NIST as public escalation baselines. Runtime must retrieve tenant legal, privacy, contractual, and jurisdiction-specific authority before any regulated communication or disclosure-adjacent output.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Vendor SIEM, EDR, identity, cloud, email, and endpoint telemetry schemas differ materially.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack defines required evidence concepts rather than vendor-specific fields. Runtime materialization must map tenant product schemas to the canonical artifact requirements before producing high-confidence recommendations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant severity labels and incident classification may not align with public guidance vocabulary.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Tenant severity matrices may be used as overlays, but cannot override hard refusal triggers or remove evidence and authority prerequisites.

notes: The research baseline covers workflow, evidence, terminology, regulated caveats, refusal triggers, and failure handling rather than generic cybersecurity best practices alone.
The lane is security-operations advisory. Execution authority, formal legal or audit authority, and specialized technical ownership remain outside the lane even when the SOC evidence is urgent.
